title: Extending Limits of United States Customs Waters Act of 2025
short_title: Extending Limits of United States Customs Waters Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill doubles the area where U.S. border patrol can chase and stop boats from twelve miles to twenty-four miles off the American coast. Congress wants to make this change because today's boats are much faster than before and can easily escape from law enforcement in the current twelve-mile zone. The new twenty-four mile limit will give border patrol agents more space and time to catch boats that might be smuggling drugs or people into the country. The bill follows international ocean rules that America already agreed to follow.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Finance.

sections.title: Section 1. Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Extending Limits of United States Customs Waters Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2. Findings; Sense of Congress
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. On December twenty seventh, nineteen eighty eight, President Reagan signed a proclamation that expanded the territorial sea of the United States from three nautical miles to twelve nautical miles from the baselines of the United States. These baselines are determined according to international law. On August second, nineteen ninety nine, President Clinton signed another proclamation that extended the contiguous zone of the United States from twelve nautical miles to twenty four nautical miles from the baselines of the United States. This zone also follows international law but cannot go into the territorial sea of another country. International law, as written in the United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea and consistent with both presidential proclamations, says that every coastal state has the right to establish its territorial sea up to twelve nautical miles from its baselines. A coastal state's contiguous zone cannot extend beyond twenty four nautical miles from the baselines used to measure the territorial sea. A coastal state has complete control over its flagged vessels within its territorial seas and on the high seas. In the contiguous zone of a coastal state, the state may exercise control to prevent violations of its customs, tax, immigration, or health laws and regulations within its territory or territorial sea. The state may also punish violations of those laws and regulations that were committed within its territory or territorial sea. International law also recognizes that outside the territorial waters of a coastal state, vessels and aircraft from all countries enjoy freedom of navigation and flight on the high seas. Under these freedoms and international law requirements, before boarding a vessel outside the territorial waters of a coastal state but within that state's contiguous zone, state authorities generally must have reasonable grounds to believe the vessel is headed to that state or has violated or is trying to violate the customs, tax, immigration, or health laws and regulations of that state. Hot pursuit of a foreign vessel may happen when competent authorities of the state have good reason to believe the vessel or one of its boats has violated the laws and regulations of that state. Hot pursuit must begin when the foreign vessel or one of its boats is within the internal waters, territorial sea, or contiguous zone of the state. It may continue outside the territorial sea or contiguous zone only if the pursuit has not been interrupted. If the foreign vessel is within the contiguous zone of the state, hot pursuit may only happen if there has been a violation of the rights that the contiguous zone was established to protect. Congress believes it is necessary to extend the authority of U.S. Customs and Border Protection to conduct law enforcement activities in the customs waters of the United States from twelve nautical miles to twenty four nautical miles. This is because modern technology continues to change and expand rapidly, and the performance and speed of maritime vessels, including those used to violate United States laws or evade United States law enforcement agents, continue to improve. The limit of twelve nautical miles no longer gives law enforcement agents enough time to stop such vessels. Congress also believes that extending the customs waters of the United States to the limits allowed by international law will help the law enforcement and public health interests of the United States.

sections.title: Section 3. Extension of Customs Waters of the United States
sections.text: This section changes two existing laws. First, it amends Section four hundred one part j of the Tariff Act of nineteen thirty. The current definition of customs waters is changed to mean, in the case of the United States, the waters within twelve nautical miles from the baselines of the United States, determined according to international law, and in the case of any other country or territory, the waters within the territorial sea of the United States to the limits allowed by international law according to Presidential Proclamation fifty nine twenty eight of December twenty seventh, nineteen eighty eight, and the contiguous zone of the United States to the limits allowed by international law according to Presidential Proclamation seventy two nineteen of September second, nineteen ninety nine. Second, it makes the same changes to Section four hundred one part c of the Anti-Smuggling Act. These amendments will take effect the day after this law is passed.
